reasoning: The market traded at 0.23 in early April, but this was prior to the surprise March 2025 CPI release, which showed a -0.1% month-over-month drop (bringing the YoY headline rate to 2.4%, below the 2.5% threshold). While this surprise meaningfully increases the chance that the end-of-year inflation rate resolves <2.5%, the underlying math makes it difficult to sustain. The sum of Q1 2025 CPI prints is roughly 0.8% (0.5% + 0.4% - 0.1%). To end 2025 with less than a 2.5% cumulative sum, the remaining 9 months must average <= 0.18% per month. Furthermore, base effects over the summer are unfavorable (May and June 2024 had 0.0% and -0.1% prints, so replacing them with even moderate positive numbers will push the YoY rate up to 2.7-2.9%). Finally, incoming tariffs pose an upward risk to prices throughout 2025. Taking the base baseline of 0.23 and upgrading it slightly to reflect the immediate benefit of the -0.1% March print, a 28% estimate appropriately balances the current 2.4% rate against the structural expectation that the rate will climb back above 2.5% by Q4.
